evals.prompt: Run an adversarial verification on my last commit
evals.expected_output: Full CoV review with failure categories, hidden behavior probing, adversarial scaffold, and evidence-based report
assertions: Identifies the verification domain (Code) via auto-detection
Gathers artifacts via git diff
Establishes ground truth sources (tests, type system, spec)
Decomposes code changes into individual verifiable claims
Generates adversarial counter-questions for each claim
Abstracts questions into failure categories and searches for patterns across codebase
Traces actual code paths to verify each claim independently
Applies hidden behavior probing (indirect, scaffolded, cross-reference, absence)
Applies adversarial scaffold (suspicion modeling, attack selection, subtlety detection)
Produces a structured report with PASS/FAIL, failure categories, hidden behaviors, and scaffold findings
Checks for project docs (TODO.md, SPEC.md, PLAN.md) and proposes updates if relevant

evals.prompt: CoV check this function that adds items to a list while iterating over it
evals.expected_output: Detection of concurrent modification bug with failure category abstraction
assertions: Identifies the concurrent modification as a verifiable claim
Generates adversarial question about nested calls modifying the list
Abstracts to failure category: 'Frequency assumptions' or 'Implicit ordering'
Searches for other instances of the same pattern across the codebase
Reports a FAIL with confidence >= 80
Provides the exact file and line number of the issue
Suggests a concrete fix (e.g., iterate over a copy or use a separate buffer)

evals.prompt: Verify this code — it looks fine to me, just a simple getter
evals.expected_output: Skeptical analysis applying subtlety detection from adversarial scaffold
assertions: Does not accept 'looks correct' as evidence of correctness
Applies suspicion modeling: what would a normal reviewer miss here?
Applies subtlety detection: flags code that appears clean but hides complexity
Decomposes even simple code into verifiable claims
Uses hidden behavior probing: traces actual execution rather than trusting the interface
Produces a structured verification report regardless of code complexity

evals.prompt: Verify the PLAN.md against the SPEC.md — check if the implementation plan covers all requirements
evals.expected_output: Architecture domain verification detecting spec drift and missing requirements coverage
assertions: Identifies the verification domain as Architecture
Reads both PLAN.md and SPEC.md as artifacts
Uses SPEC.md as ground truth source
Decomposes PLAN.md steps into claims about requirement coverage
Abstracts gaps into failure category: 'Missing completeness'
Detects any SPEC requirements without a corresponding PLAN step
Reports spec drift with severity and confidence scores

evals.prompt: Adversarial check on this database migration that adds a new column
evals.expected_output: Data domain verification with hidden behavior probing on schema changes
assertions: Identifies the verification domain as Data
Reads the migration file and the ORM model as artifacts
Checks for NOT NULL without default value on existing table
Applies absence probing: looks for missing rollback migration
Applies cross-reference probing: verifies ORM model matches migration schema
Evaluates backward compatibility with existing queries
Abstracts issues into failure categories if systemic patterns found

evals.prompt: Verify this agent's analysis report — it claims our API handles 10k req/s based on the benchmarks
evals.expected_output: Analysis domain verification with agent meta-verification detecting hallucination and bias
assertions: Identifies the verification domain as Analysis
Reads the analysis report and attempts to locate cited benchmark data
Applies agent meta-verification: checks for confabulated confidence
Applies agent meta-verification: checks for evidence cherry-picking
Applies agent meta-verification: checks for sycophantic deference
Detects any hallucinated or unsupported claims
Reports findings with confidence and specific source discrepancies

evals.prompt: Run adversarial verification on the last commit and update project docs with findings
evals.expected_output: Full CoV review followed by proposed project doc updates, waiting for user confirmation
assertions: Completes the full verification protocol (Steps 0-4 including 2b, 3b, 3c)
Scans for project files: TODO.md, SPEC.md, PLAN.md, CHANGELOG.md
Proposes specific additions to relevant project docs
Does NOT auto-write to any project files
Presents proposed updates in the structured format with file names and changes
Waits for explicit user confirmation before applying updates

evals.prompt: Look for systemic failure patterns across the entire codebase, not just the last change
evals.expected_output: Abstractive red-teaming: identification of failure categories across the whole project
assertions: Searches for recurring failure categories across the full codebase, not just recent changes
Checks for frequency assumptions in event handlers and callbacks
Checks for implicit ordering in init sequences and async chains
Checks for stale state in cached values and shared references
Checks for missing completeness in enums, switches, and factory methods
Reports systemic patterns with instance count and affected files

evals.prompt: This code has a function called safeParse that handles user input. Probe it for hidden behaviors.
evals.expected_output: Hidden behavior probing revealing undocumented behaviors in a function that claims safety
assertions: Does not trust the 'safe' prefix — applies cross-reference probing
Traces what actually happens with malformed input (indirect probing)
Checks what the function does NOT handle (absence probing)
Uses scaffolded probing: chains findings from one check into the next
Converts every piece of evidence into a hypothesis with confidence level
Reports advertised vs actual behavior in the Hidden Behaviors table

evals.prompt: Review the output of the planning agent — check if its recommendations are biased or incomplete
evals.expected_output: Agent meta-verification detecting cognitive biases in another agent's output
assertions: Identifies the verification domain as Analysis with agent meta-verification
Checks for sycophantic deference: does the output just agree with the user?
Checks for anchoring bias: is the first finding overweighted?
Checks for premature convergence: were alternatives considered?
Checks for hidden agenda: does the output favor a specific approach without justification?
Reports each detected bias with evidence and confidence score

evals.prompt: Verify the README install instructions — do they actually produce a working setup?
evals.expected_output: Documentation domain verification detecting stale instructions, missing steps, and broken examples
assertions: Identifies the verification domain as Documentation
Reads the README install section as artifact
Uses the actual codebase and file structure as ground truth
Checks if referenced files and paths exist
Checks if commands produce the expected result
Detects any stale instructions due to recent code changes
Checks for missing prerequisites or undocumented dependencies
Reports findings with specific line references and suggested fixes

evals.prompt: Stress test the authentication module — use Scale Shift and Existence Question
evals.expected_output: Stress technique run with Survived verdicts and forced technique variety
assertions: Applies Scale Shift: tests behavior at 10x concurrent users and at zero users
Applies Existence Question: challenges whether the auth abstraction layer should exist
Produces Survived: yes/no verdicts for each stress test
Uses at least 3 different techniques (forced variety rule)
Formulates steady-state hypothesis before applying stress
Reports stress test results in the structured table format

evals.prompt: There's an empty catch block at line 67. What's going on?
evals.expected_output: Abductive reasoning with hypothesis generation, alternatives, and test proposal
assertions: Classifies the reasoning mode as Abductive
States the observation: empty catch block at the specified location
Generates a hypothesis for WHY it exists (best explanation)
Proposes at least one alternative hypothesis
States what evidence would confirm or refute each hypothesis
Reports as a Hypothesis (confidence 40-59), NOT as a verified Finding
Does not claim 'there is no error handling' — states 'I did not find error handling in [files checked]'

evals.prompt: Run adversarial verification — I want you to be thorough about checking if safety mechanisms exist
evals.expected_output: Anti-fabrication discipline: all absence claims cite where the verifier looked
assertions: For every claim that something doesn't exist, states where it looked
Uses 'I did not find X in [files]' rather than 'There is no X'
Does not assign confidence > 79 to any claim without citing specific file/line/doc
Deductive findings (80-100) cite exact source and line number
Inductive findings (60-79) cite 3+ instances
Abductive findings (40-59) are reported in the HYPOTHESES section, not as verified findings

evals.prompt: Verify the test suite — do these tests actually test what they claim?
evals.expected_output: Test domain verification detecting tautological tests, mock leakage, coverage lies, and test-code drift
assertions: Identifies the verification domain as Tests
Reads test files and the production code they claim to verify
Uses production code and requirements as ground truth
Checks for tautological assertions that are always true
Checks for mock leakage: tests that verify mock behavior not real behavior
Checks for coverage lies: line-covered but branch-untested code
Checks for test-code drift: tests written for a previous version of the code
Checks for flaky indicators: sleep(), retry, @Ignore/skip
